title: __post_init__ Processing

glossary.definition: Adjusting or changing data so it follows a standard, consistent format, like converting all text to lowercase.
A specific place or method provided by a system that allows you to insert your own custom code to run automatically.

overview.what: The `__post_init__` method is a special method in dataclasses that runs immediately after the auto-generated `__init__` finishes. It allows you to execute custom logic like validating data, normalizing inputs (e.g., lowercasing strings), or computing dependent fields (fields whose values depend on other fields). Mental model: `__init__` puts the raw data into the box, and `__post_init__` inspects or modifies the box before handing it back to the caller.
overview.why: Because `@dataclass` generates `__init__` for you, you can't easily add validation or compute fields inside the constructor. `__post_init__` provides a hook to perform these actions automatically every time an instance is created.

why_learning_this: You can validate data at creation time rather than dealing with invalid state later.
You can auto-calculate fields based on the raw inputs provided by the user.

common_mistakes.title: Forgetting to type-hint the derived field
common_mistakes.explanation: If you want a field to be computed in `__post_init__`, it still needs to be declared as a dataclass field. To avoid requiring it in `__init__`, you must use `field(init=False)`.

common_mistakes.title: Assigning to fields in a frozen dataclass
common_mistakes.explanation: In a `frozen=True` dataclass, you cannot assign `self.attr = value` in `__post_init__`. You must use `object.__setattr__(self, 'attr', value)` instead.

recall_questions.answer: It executes immediately after the automatically generated `__init__` method finishes.

recall_questions.q: What are two common use cases for `__post_init__`?
recall_questions.answer: Validating the input data (e.g., ensuring a number is positive) and computing/deriving the values of dependent fields.

recall_questions.q: If you want a field to be populated in `__post_init__` but not required during initialization, how do you define it?
recall_questions.answer: By using `dataclasses.field(init=False)` when defining the class attribute.

understanding_checks.question: What is printed when this code is executed?
understanding_checks.why: The auto-generated `__init__` sets `self.price = -50`. Then `__post_init__` runs, sees the negative price, and overwrites it with `0.0`.

understanding_checks.question: Why does this code fail to run as intended?
understanding_checks.why: Because `area` is defined as a normal field, the auto-generated `__init__` requires it to be passed. To compute it in `__post_init__` without requiring it, it should be defined with `field(init=False)`.

code_walkthrough.focus: self.is_admin = ... calculates the dependent field after username is set.

aha_moment.prediction: Will the 'Validating...' print statement execute if you create a valid Rectangle?
aha_moment.common_guess: No, or I have to call validate() manually.
aha_moment.why: Yes, it prints 'Validating 10x5'. `__post_init__` is guaranteed to run automatically at the very end of instantiation, intercepting and verifying the data before the caller receives the object.

practice_tasks.title: Normalize the Username
practice_tasks.prompt: Given this dataclass, modify it by adding a `__post_init__` method that normalizes `username` to be entirely lowercase.

challenge.title: Data Validation on Init
challenge.prompt: Create a dataclass `Temperature` that takes one float field `celsius`. Implement `__post_init__` to raise a `ValueError` if `celsius` is below absolute zero (-273.15).
